name: Saudi Arabia
tagline: From the incense roads of ancient Arabia and the cradle of Islam to the oil-fueled kingdom of the Al Saud.
summary: Saudi Arabia occupies the heart of the Arabian Peninsula, a land of deserts and oases that gave rise to ancient caravan kingdoms, became the birthplace of Islam in the 7th century, and was unified into a single modern state in 1932. The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia was proclaimed by Abdulaziz ibn Saud after a thirty-year campaign that began with his capture of Riyadh in 1902. Its roots reach back to the religious and political alliance forged in 1744 at Diriyah between the Al Saud and the reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab. The discovery of oil in 1938 transformed a poor pilgrimage economy into one of the world's largest energy exporters, and the kingdom remains custodian of Islam's holiest cities, Mecca and Medina.

founding.label: Proclamation of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
founding.detail: On 23 September 1932 the regions of Najd and the Hejaz, conquered and consolidated over three decades by Abdulaziz ibn Saud (Ibn Saud), were merged and renamed the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia by royal decree. The kingdom's deeper origins lie in the birth of Islam in the Hejaz in the 7th century, when the Prophet Muhammad preached in Mecca and Medina, and in the First Saudi State founded in 1744 at Diriyah through the alliance between the Al Saud ruler Muhammad ibn Saud and the religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab.

quickFacts.label: Official name
quickFacts.value: Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (al-Mamlaka al-Arabiyya al-Saudiyya)

quickFacts.label: National Day
quickFacts.value: 23 September, marking the 1932 proclamation of the kingdom

quickFacts.label: Founding dynasty
quickFacts.value: House of Saud (Al Saud), ruling since the First Saudi State of 1744

quickFacts.label: Holiest sites
quickFacts.value: Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques in Mecca and Medina

quickFacts.label: Predominant religion
quickFacts.value: Islam; birthplace of the faith and of the Prophet Muhammad

quickFacts.label: Economic mainstay
quickFacts.value: Petroleum, first discovered in commercial quantities in 1938

eras.title: Ancient Arabia and the Caravan Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: Long before Islam, the Arabian Peninsula was crossed by incense roads that sustained wealthy oasis kingdoms and linked southern Arabia to the Mediterranean and Mesopotamia.
body: The deserts of Arabia were never empty. From the third millennium BCE the trading culture of Dilmun, centered on the eastern Gulf, connected Mesopotamia with the Indus valley, and Sumerian texts remembered it as a near-paradisal land of prosperity. Across the peninsula, settled communities clustered around oases and wells, while pastoral nomads moved between them.

From the first millennium BCE the overland incense trade became the engine of wealth in the region. Frankincense and myrrh from southern Arabia were carried north by camel caravan along routes that passed through the northwest of the peninsula. The kingdom of Lihyan, centered on Dadan (modern Dedan/AlUla), grew rich on this trade, developing its own script, monumental art, and irrigated agriculture.

By the late first century BCE the Nabataeans, master traders and hydraulic engineers based at Petra, extended their control southward and made Hegra (al-Hijr, also called Madain Salih) their second city. Under King Aretas IV (9 BCE - 40 CE) Hegra flourished, its sandstone cliffs carved with more than a hundred monumental tombs. After Rome annexed the Nabataean kingdom in 106 CE, the caravan cities slowly declined, but they left a vivid archaeological record of pre-Islamic Arabia.

events.title: The Dilmun trading culture flourishes
events.summary: Around the eastern shores of the Arabian Peninsula, the Dilmun culture became a hub linking Mesopotamia with the Indus valley, remembered in Sumerian literature as a land of prosperity and long life.

events.title: The kingdom of Lihyan rises at Dadan
events.summary: The Lihyanite kingdom, centered on Dadan in the AlUla region, grew wealthy from the incense trade and tribute, developing its own language, script, and irrigated agriculture.

events.title: The Nabataeans make Hegra their southern capital
events.summary: The Nabataean kingdom extended south and developed Hegra (al-Hijr) as a major caravan city, carving monumental rock-cut tombs that survive as the second-largest Nabataean site after Petra.

events.title: Rome annexes the Nabataean kingdom
events.summary: The Roman Empire absorbed the Nabataean realm, after which Hegra and the northern caravan cities gradually declined as trade routes shifted.

figures.name: Aretas IV
figures.blurb: The most prominent Nabataean monarch, under whom Hegra reached its peak as a caravan city and the great rock-cut tombs were carved.

pullquote.text: The desert kingdoms of Arabia grew rich not from the land but from the road, taxing the frankincense that crossed it.

eras.title: The Birth of Islam: Muhammad, Mecca and Medina
eras.standfirst: In the early 7th century the Prophet Muhammad began preaching a strict monotheism in Mecca; his migration to Medina in 622 founded the first Muslim community and reshaped world history.
body: Muhammad was born in Mecca around 570 CE into the Banu Hashim clan of the dominant Quraysh tribe, in a Hejaz where the Kaaba was already a center of pilgrimage and trade. According to Islamic tradition, at about the age of forty he began to receive revelations through the angel Gabriel in a cave on Mount Hira, calling his people to worship one God and warning of a final judgment.

His message of monotheism and social justice met resistance from the Meccan elite, whose authority and trade were bound up with the existing shrine. After roughly thirteen years of preaching and persecution, in 622 CE Muhammad and his followers migrated to the oasis city of Yathrib, soon renamed Medina. This Hijra became the founding event of the Muslim community and marks year one of the Islamic calendar.

In Medina, Muhammad united the local clans and his followers into a single political and religious community, negotiating an agreement among them. Over the following decade the new faith spread across the Hejaz; Mecca submitted, and by Muhammad's death in 632 CE much of the Arabian Peninsula had embraced Islam. The two cities of Mecca and Medina have remained the holiest sites of Islam ever since, and the land of their origin lies at the spiritual heart of modern Saudi Arabia.

events.title: Birth of Muhammad in Mecca
events.summary: Muhammad was born in Mecca into the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh, the tribe that controlled the pilgrimage sanctuary of the Kaaba.

events.title: The first revelations
events.summary: According to Islamic tradition Muhammad, at about forty, began receiving revelations through the angel Gabriel, proclaiming a strict monotheism that would form the Quran.

events.title: The Hijra to Medina
events.summary: Facing persecution, Muhammad and his followers migrated from Mecca to Yathrib (Medina), founding the first Muslim community; the event marks year one of the Islamic calendar.

events.title: The submission of Mecca
events.summary: Muhammad returned to Mecca largely without bloodshed, cleansed the Kaaba of idols, and established Islam across much of the Hejaz.

events.title: Death of Muhammad
events.summary: By the Prophet's death much of the Arabian Peninsula had embraced Islam, leaving Mecca and Medina as the enduring holy cities of the faith.

figures.name: Muhammad
figures.blurb: Founder of Islam, born in Mecca, whose preaching of monotheism and migration to Medina in 622 created the first Muslim community and reshaped the history of Arabia and the wider world.

pullquote.text: The migration to Medina in 622 marks not only the start of the Muslim calendar but the birth of a community that would transform Arabia.

eras.title: The Caliphates and the Hejaz under Outside Rule
eras.standfirst: After Muhammad's death the centers of Islamic power moved away from Arabia, and for more than a thousand years the Hejaz and its holy cities were governed by distant caliphs, local sharifs, and finally the Ottomans.
body: Under the first caliphs Medina served as the capital of a rapidly expanding empire, but within a generation the political center of Islam shifted outward. The Umayyad caliphs (661-750) ruled from Damascus and the Abbasids (from 750) from Baghdad, governing the Hejaz through appointed governors while lavishing patronage on Mecca and Medina to secure the prestige of the pilgrimage.

From the 10th century onward the holy cities were administered locally by the Sharifate of Mecca, a line of sharifs claiming descent from the Prophet, who held real authority over the Hejaz while acknowledging the nominal sovereignty of whichever greater power dominated the Islamic world. The interior of the peninsula, Najd, remained a patchwork of tribal and oasis principalities largely beyond outside control.

In 1517 the Ottomans, having defeated the Mamluks, gained suzerainty over the Hejaz; the Sharif of Mecca acknowledged the Ottoman sultan as caliph, and Ottoman authority over the holy cities remained largely indirect, leaving day-to-day power with the sharifs. This long era of fragmented and external rule set the stage for the religious revival movement that would emerge from Najd in the 18th century and give rise to the first Saudi state.

events.title: Medina as capital of the early caliphate
events.summary: Under the first (Rashidun) caliphs, Medina served as the capital of an expanding Muslim empire before power shifted away from Arabia.

events.title: Umayyad and Abbasid rule over the Hejaz
events.summary: The Umayyads from Damascus and the Abbasids from Baghdad governed the Hejaz through appointed governors, patronizing Mecca and Medina from afar.

events.title: The Sharifate of Mecca takes shape
events.summary: A line of sharifs descended from the Prophet came to administer Mecca and the Hejaz, holding local authority for nearly a thousand years under shifting overlords.

events.title: Ottoman suzerainty over the Hejaz
events.summary: After defeating the Mamluks, the Ottomans gained sovereignty over the holy cities; the Sharif of Mecca recognized the Ottoman sultan as caliph while retaining local power.

pullquote.text: For a thousand years the holy cities were ruled from afar, while the deserts of Najd kept their own counsel.

eras.title: The First and Second Saudi States and the Al Saud-Wahhab Alliance
eras.standfirst: In 1744 a pact between the ruler of Diriyah and a puritan reformer fused religious revival with political ambition, creating the first state of the House of Saud and the religious-political partnership at the kingdom's foundation.
body: In 1744 in the oasis town of Diriyah in central Najd, the local ruler Muhammad ibn Saud gave refuge and support to the religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who preached a return to a strict, unadorned monotheism purged of what he saw as later accretions. Their alliance, sealed by the Diriyah pact, joined the political authority of the Al Saud to the religious mission of the reform movement, and this partnership has underpinned Saudi statehood ever since.

The First Saudi State expanded rapidly from Diriyah, bringing much of the Arabian Peninsula under its control and, in the early 19th century, taking Mecca and Medina. This challenge to the Ottoman order provoked a response: between 1811 and 1818 the Egyptian forces of Muhammad Ali Pasha, acting for the sultan, campaigned in Arabia. Ibrahim Pasha besieged Diriyah, which surrendered in September 1818 and was systematically razed, and the last Saudi imam was sent to Constantinople and executed.

The dynasty soon recovered. In 1824 Turki ibn Abdullah Al Saud expelled the occupying forces and re-established Saudi rule with Riyadh as the new capital, founding the Second Saudi State. This realm, narrower than the first, was weakened by internal succession struggles within the Al Saud and by rivalry with the Al Rashid of Hail. In 1891 the Al Rashid overran the Saudi domain, drove the family into exile in Kuwait, and brought the second state to an end, setting the scene for its dramatic 20th-century revival.

events.title: The Diriyah pact founds the First Saudi State
events.summary: Muhammad ibn Saud, ruler of Diriyah, and the reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab formed an alliance joining political power and religious revival, the foundation of the first Saudi state.

events.title: The Ottoman-Egyptian campaign against the Saudis
events.summary: On behalf of the Ottoman sultan, the Egyptian forces of Muhammad Ali Pasha waged war on the expanding First Saudi State, driving it from the holy cities and into the Najdi heartland.

events.title: The fall and destruction of Diriyah
events.summary: After a months-long siege Ibrahim Pasha took Diriyah in September 1818 and razed it, ending the First Saudi State; the last imam was executed in Constantinople.

events.title: Turki ibn Abdullah founds the Second Saudi State
events.summary: Turki ibn Abdullah Al Saud expelled the occupying garrisons, re-established Saudi rule, and made Riyadh the capital of a revived but smaller state.

events.title: The Al Rashid end the Second Saudi State
events.summary: Weakened by internal feuds, the second state fell to the rival Al Rashid of Hail, who drove the Al Saud into exile in Kuwait.

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.blurb: Najdi theologian whose call for a return to strict monotheism, allied with the Al Saud in 1744, became the religious foundation of the Saudi states.

figures.name: Turki ibn Abdullah Al Saud
figures.role: Founder of the Second Saudi State
figures.blurb: Expelled the occupying forces in 1824 and re-established Saudi rule with Riyadh as its capital, restoring the dynasty after the destruction of Diriyah.

pullquote.text: The pact sealed at Diriyah in 1744 bound throne and pulpit together, and that bond has outlasted every fall of the dynasty.

eras.title: Unification and the Founding of the Kingdom (1902-1932)
eras.standfirst: A daring raid on Riyadh in 1902 launched Abdulaziz ibn Saud on a thirty-year campaign that reunited Najd and the Hejaz into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
body: In January 1902 the young Abdulaziz ibn Saud, returning from exile in Kuwait with a small band of followers, stormed the Masmak fortress in Riyadh and seized the city from the Al Rashid. This audacious recapture of the family's ancestral capital restored the House of Saud to Najd and began the campaign that would build a unified Saudi state.

Over the following decades Abdulaziz extended his control across central and eastern Arabia, drawing on the military zeal of the Ikhwan, tribal warriors loyal to the religious cause. In 1925 his forces took Mecca and Medina, ending the rule of the Hashemite Sharif Hussein in the Hejaz. In 1927 the Treaty of Jeddah saw Britain formally recognize the independence and sovereignty of his realm, then styled the Kingdom of the Hejaz and Nejd.

On 23 September 1932 the two principal regions were merged and proclaimed the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, with Abdulaziz as its first king. The new state was poor, its economy resting on pilgrimage revenues and trade, but it was unified, internationally recognized, and ruled by a single dynasty for the first time in centuries. Within a few years the discovery of oil would utterly transform its fortunes.

events.title: Abdulaziz captures Riyadh
events.summary: Returning from exile, Abdulaziz ibn Saud and a small force seized the Masmak fortress in Riyadh from the Al Rashid, launching the campaign to rebuild the Saudi state.

events.title: Conquest of the Hejaz and the holy cities
events.summary: Abdulaziz's forces took Mecca and Medina, ending Hashemite rule in the Hejaz and bringing the holy cities under Saudi control.

events.title: The Treaty of Jeddah
events.summary: Britain formally recognized the independence and sovereignty of Ibn Saud's Kingdom of the Hejaz and Nejd, securing its international standing.

events.title: Proclamation of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
events.summary: By royal decree the regions of Najd and the Hejaz were unified and renamed the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, with Abdulaziz ibn Saud as its first king.

figures.name: Abdulaziz ibn Saud (Ibn Saud)
figures.role: Founder and first King of Saudi Arabia
figures.blurb: Recaptured Riyadh in 1902 and, over three decades of conquest and diplomacy, unified the Arabian Peninsula, proclaiming the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932 and reigning until his death.

pullquote.text: What began with sixty men scaling a fortress wall in Riyadh ended, thirty years later, in the founding of a kingdom.

eras.title: Oil, Modernization and Contemporary Saudi Arabia
eras.standfirst: The discovery of oil in 1938 turned a poor desert kingdom into a global energy power, financing rapid modernization while the Al Saud navigated regional crises and, more recently, an ambitious drive to diversify beyond oil.
body: In 1933 Abdulaziz granted an oil concession to the Standard Oil Company of California, and after years of exploration commercial quantities of oil were struck at Dammam Well No. 7 in 1938. The first crude was exported in 1939, and the venture grew into the Arabian American Oil Company (Aramco). Oil revenues transformed state finances, funding roads, schools, hospitals, and a vast expansion of the cities, while the kingdom retained its conservative religious character and its role as custodian of Mecca and Medina.

Wealth brought influence. Under King Faisal, Saudi Arabia led the Arab members of OPEC in the 1973 oil embargo against states supporting Israel, quadrupling oil prices and demonstrating the kingdom's leverage over the world economy. In the decades that followed the kingdom became a pillar of the global oil market, a major regional power, and at times a flashpoint, as in the seizure of the Grand Mosque in 1979 and the basing of foreign troops during the 1990-91 Gulf crisis.

Heritage and identity have also been reclaimed: Hegra, the great Nabataean site, became Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2008, followed by At-Turaif in Diriyah, cradle of the first Saudi state, in 2010. In 2016 Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman launched Vision 2030, a sweeping program to diversify the economy away from oil, expand the private sector and tourism, and reshape Saudi society. The kingdom of the 21st century remains defined by the interplay of oil wealth, dynastic rule, and its singular place at the heart of the Islamic world.

events.title: The oil concession is granted
events.summary: Abdulaziz granted an oil exploration concession to the Standard Oil Company of California, the agreement that would become the basis of Aramco.

events.title: Oil struck at Dammam Well No. 7
events.summary: Commercial quantities of oil were discovered at Dammam Well No. 7 in March 1938, the find that transformed Saudi Arabia's economy and the world energy market.

events.title: The Arab oil embargo
events.summary: Under King Faisal, Saudi Arabia led Arab OPEC states in an embargo against supporters of Israel, sharply raising oil prices and demonstrating the kingdom's economic power.

events.title: Hegra becomes a UNESCO World Heritage Site
events.summary: The Nabataean site of Hegra (al-Hijr) was inscribed as Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognizing the kingdom's deep pre-Islamic past.

events.title: Vision 2030 is launched
events.summary: Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman unveiled Vision 2030, a program to diversify the economy beyond oil, grow the private sector and tourism, and reshape Saudi society.

figures.name: King Faisal
figures.blurb: Modernizing monarch who led the 1973 oil embargo and raised Saudi Arabia to the status of a world power before his assassination in 1975.

figures.name: Mohammed bin Salman
figures.blurb: Architect of Vision 2030, the 2016 program to diversify the economy beyond oil and transform Saudi society, and the dominant figure in 21st-century Saudi politics.

pullquote.text: Oil made the kingdom wealthy; Vision 2030 is its wager that the future must be built on more than oil.
